source.title: A synthetic microbial biosensor for high-throughput screening of lactam biocatalysts

initial_request.research_problem: Develop a high-throughput screening biosensor for lactam biocatalysts to enable discovery of enzymes that cyclize ω-amino fatty acids into lactams.
initial_request.known_context: Lactams are important industrial chemicals; existing screening methods are low-throughput. NitR from Alcaligenes faecalis is a known lactam-responsive transcription factor.
quantifiable_goals: Enable screening of large metagenomic libraries

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No high-throughput biosensor for lactam biocatalysts exists.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can NitR from Alcaligenes faecalis be used as a specific lactam-responsive transcription factor in a biosensor?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: NitR can be used as a specific lactam-responsive TF in a biosensor.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test NitR responsiveness to ε-caprolactam and other effectors.
evolution_trajectory.observation: NitR is known to regulate nitrile degradation operon; hypothesized to respond to lactams.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to construct a genetic circuit with NitR and a reporter to test responsiveness.

evidence.quote_or_span: we examined the applicability of the A. faecalis nitR regulatory subunit (nitrile degradation operon) in the development of the CL-GESS by assessing the induction of nitA gene expression in the presence of various effector molecules.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Initial genetic circuit with NitR and reporter may not be optimal.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the minimal functional P_nitA promoter and optimal expression level of NitR?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Truncating P_nitA and optimizing promoter/RBS for NitR will improve sensor performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform promoter truncation and combinatorial optimization of promoter/RBS.

parameters.promoters: BioBrick promoters with known relative strength

evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified minimal functional P_nitA (v3) and best combination J23114-B0034 (v4) with highest fold change.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize sensitivity and specificity of optimized sensor v4.

evidence.quote_or_span: Systematic combinatorial analysis and optimization of NitR expression was carried out using constitutive BioBrick promoters with known relative strength

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Optimized sensor v4 constructed but not characterized.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the detection limit, linear range, and specificity of CL-GESSv4?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: CL-GESSv4 will detect ε-caprolactam with high sensitivity and specificity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure fluorescence response to varying concentrations of ε-caprolactam and test specificity against related compounds.
evolution_trajectory.observation: CL-GESSv4 detected ε-caprolactam down to 500 μM with linear response up to 30 mM; showed specificity for lactams and related compounds but not biosynthetic intermediates.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Sensitivity needs improvement for screening applications; consider directed evolution of NitR.

evidence.quote_or_span: The minimal concentration of ε-caprolactam required to activate NitR was 500 μM; moreover, a tight correlation was observed between fluorescence intensity and ε-caprolactam concentration.

CL-GESSv4 sensed ε-caprolactam, ε-caprolactone, cyclohexanone, N-acetylcaprolactam, δ-valerolactam, δ-valerolactone, benzonitrile, and isovaleronitrile, whereas detected none of the intermediates in the proposed ε-caprolactam biosynthesis pathway

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CL-GESSv4 sensitivity is limited to 500 μM.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can directed evolution of NitR improve sensor sensitivity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR mutagenesis of NitR followed by FACS screening will yield mutants with improved sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct error-prone PCR library of NitR and perform three rounds of FACS screening (positive, negative, positive).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Library constructed and screened; putative hits selected after three rounds.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize isolated mutants for improved sensitivity.

evidence.quote_or_span: After three rounds of screening, the highest 0.5% of phenotypes were selected as putative hits
A NitR mutant library was constructed by error-prone PCR using a PCR mutagenesis kit with a mutation rate of 2–4 mutations per 1000 base pairs and using CL-GESS as a template.
Approximately 20,000 cells with high fluorescence intensity (top 0.4%) in 5.6 × 10⁶ cells were collected

In the second round of screening, false-positive cells showing high fluorescence intensity in the absence of ε-caprolactam were removed by sorting non-fluorescent cells. Approximately 1.5 × 10⁵ (bottom 3%) non-fluorescent cells in 3 × 10⁶ negatively sorted cells were collected
The minimal concentration of ε-caprolactam required to activate NitR was 500 μM; moreover, a tight correlation was observed between fluorescence intensity and ε-caprolactam concentration.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Putative NitR mutants isolated from directed evolution.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which mutant provides the best sensitivity improvement?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: L117F mutant will show improved sensitivity with lower background.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize L117F and other mutants for fluorescence response and background.
evolution_trajectory.observation: L117F mutant exhibited strong fluorescence with 11.4-fold increase and lower background compared to L117W; chosen for further use.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use L117F sensor for metagenomic screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Therefore, we used L117F mutant for the screening of ε-caprolactam producing enzymes because high background of L117W mutant could be a problem during the screening of large library.

Interestingly, the substitution of L117 with aromatic residues, as in CL- $GESS_{NitR-L117F}$ , CL- $GESS_{NitR-L117Y}$ , and CL- $GESS_{NitR-L117W}$ , induced a marked increase in the fluorescence signal of 11.4-fold, 5.
The single-point mutant CL- $GESS_{NitR-L117F}$ exhibited strong fluorescence in the presence of $\varepsilon$ -caprolactam (Fig.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can CL-GESS_L117F be used to screen a metagenomic library for cyclase activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Metagenomic screening with CL-GESS_L117F will identify clones that convert 6-ACA to ε-caprolactam.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen metagenomic library on agar plates containing 6-ACA and evaluate fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified 27 hits; fosmid clone #3 showed strongest fluorescence, indicating conversion of 6-ACA to ε-caprolactam.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify the gene responsible for cyclization activity in fosmid #3.

evidence.quote_or_span: one colony (named fosmid #3), with the strongest fluorescence, was selected by microscopy (Fig. 4a)

For functional screening of cyclase, E. coli EPI300 cells harboring the metagenomic library were electrophoretically transformed with the CL-GESS plasmid and were grown on LB agar containing 50 mM 6-ACA
The fluorescence intensity of colonies was evaluated by fluorescence microscopy, and colonies with strong signals were selected
4 HTS of cyclases from metagenome library using CL- $GESS_{L117F}$ .

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Fosmid #3 contains a 31-kb DNA fragment with multiple ORFs.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which ORF in fosmid #3 encodes the cyclase?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: One of the 11 ORFs in fosmid #3 encodes an enzyme that converts 6-ACA to ε-caprolactam.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Subclone 11 ORFs into pET28a(+) and test each for cyclization activity using CL-GESS_L117F.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ORF encoding 3-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase from Citrobacter freundii (CF3HBD) was positive for cyclization activity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate cyclization activity of purified CF3HBD.

evidence.quote_or_span: The DNA from fosmid #3 contained a 31-kb DNA fragment that showed up to 88% identity to Citrobacter genome sequences
we selected 11 ORFs that were up to 500 bp (Supplementary Table 4) and subcloned them into the pET28a(+) plasmid
an ORF encoding 3-hydroxybutyrate dehydrogenase from Citrobacter freundii (CF3HBD) was positive for this activity
b Analysis of fluorescence in a clone with 11 ORFs in pET28a(+) from a metagenomic fosmid using CL- $GESS_{L117F}$ to assess $\varepsilon$ -caprolactam-converting activity from 10 mM 6-ACA over 24 h.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does purified CF3HBD catalyze cyclization of 6-ACA to ε-caprolactam without cofactors?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Purified CF3HBD will convert 6-ACA to ε-caprolactam in the absence of cofactors.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Purify CF3HBD and perform in vitro cyclization assay; confirm product by LC-MS, NMR, IR.

tool_or_method.name: In vitro enzyme assay and product characterization

evolution_trajectory.observation: Purified CF3HBD catalyzed cyclization of 6-ACA to ε-caprolactam without cofactors; product purity >99% confirmed by LC-MS.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Explore sequence homologs for cyclization activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: this reaction was performed using purified enzyme and in the absence of any cofactors, such as ATP and NADH
LC-MS analysis revealed that the purity of the obtained caprolactam was >99%
For CF3HBD cyclization activity, 1 mM 6-ACA in 50 mM HEPES (pH 7.5) was reacted at 35°C for 10 min.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do other 3HBD homologs also have cyclization activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Homologs with high sequence identity to CF3HBD will also cyclize 6-ACA.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test cyclization activity of four 3HBD homologs with varying sequence identity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two homologs (SM3HBD, ES3HBD) with high identity showed cyclization activity; two with lower identity did not.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Determine crystal structure of CF3HBD to understand mechanism.

evidence.quote_or_span: CF3HBD showed 81%, 82%, 81%, 56%, and 28% sequence identity with 3HBD from Serratia marcescens, Klebsiella pneumoniae, Enterobacter sp., Rhodobacter sphaeroides, and Pseudomonas lemoignei, respectively
both enzymes showed cyclization activity towards 6-ACA to produce ε-caprolactam as well

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which active-site residues are essential for cyclization and dehydrogenase activities?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Alanine substitution of key residues will identify catalytic residues and potentially improve cyclization activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform alanine scanning mutagenesis of 10 active-site residues and measure activities.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Q91A, S139A, H141A abolished both activities; Y152A increased cyclization activity 3-fold.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Determine kinetic parameters of Y152A mutant.

evidence.quote_or_span: Alanine substitution of the catalytic residues, such as Q91, S139, and H141 completely abolished catalytic activity towards both 3-hydroxybutyric acid and 6-ACA
the Y152A mutant showed the highest cyclization activity, with a three-fold increase, for 6-ACA
Alanine substitution mutants of 10 residues located within 4.0 Å of the center of the docked 6-ACA in the crystal structure were studied

the mutant enzymes of Q91, S139, and H141 completely lost catalytic activities towards 3-hydroxybutyric acid (for dehydrogenation) and 6-ACA (for cyclization), suggesting that both activities share the catalytic site

NitR mutants (S133P, L117F, L117A, L117E, L117Q, L117R, L117W, and L117Y) and 3HBD mutants (Q91A, Q91E, S139A, S139E, V140A, V140E, H141A, H141E, K149A, K149E, Y152A, Y152E, Q193A, Q193E, Q193E, W184A, W184E, and V190A) were generated using the QuikChange II XL Site-Directed Mutagenesis kit and a primer pair (Supplementary Tables 6 and 7).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Y152A mutant shows improved cyclization activity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the kinetic parameters of wild-type and Y152A CF3HBD toward 6-ACA?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Y152A will have improved kcat compared to wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure kinetic parameters (Km, kcat) for wild-type and Y152A using 6-ACA as substrate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Y152A showed Km 2.9 mM (vs 3.3 mM WT) and kcat 208.5e-3 min-1 (vs 72.2e-3 min-1 WT), indicating improved catalytic efficiency.

evidence.quote_or_span: Table 3 Kinetic parameters of the wild-type and Y152A mutant enzymes toward 6-aminocaproic acid
In the kinetics study, various amounts of 6-ACA (0.1–20 mM) were incubated in 50 mM HEPES (pH 7.5) containing purified 3HBD enzyme at 35°C for 10 min.
Thus, we determined the kinetic parameters using the Michaelis-Menten and Lineweaver-Burk plots of the wild-type and Y152A-mutant enzymes toward 6-ACA (Supplementary Fig.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of the CL-GESS biosensor?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low background signal may limit sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note the limitation of low background signal.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Low background signal persists in CL-GESS, possibly due to leaky expression or transcriptional noise; limits sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Future work could address this limitation.

evidence.quote_or_span: there was a low background signal that was likely due to leaky reporter expression or some other factor

metrics: Detection limit: 500 μM ε-caprolactam (v4), improved to 50 μM with double mutant
Kinetic parameters: kcat 208.5e-3 min-1 for Y152A
success_verification.final_conclusion: A synthetic microbial biosensor (CL-GESS) was developed and optimized through promoter engineering and directed evolution of NitR, enabling high-throughput screening of metagenomic libraries. This led to the discovery of CF3HBD, a novel cyclase that converts 6-ACA to ε-caprolactam without cofactors. Structural and mutagenesis studies identified key catalytic residues and yielded a Y152A mutant with 3-fold improved activity.
limitations: Low background signal persists in CL-GESS, possibly due to leaky expression or transcriptional noise, limiting sensitivity.
